agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The decoupling is the whole clue.** Cost is up sharply while *ingested* volume is flat. So the driver cannot be anything that scales with ingest throughput — it has to be a cost component that grows on some *other* axis. That immediately reframes which line items matter:

- **Dedup / ingest compute** — the majority of the *compute* bill, but it's a single-threaded pass over *incoming* lines, so its cost scales with **ingest volume, which is flat**. Whatever it costs, it isn't what's *climbing*. It's a big steady cost, not the growth.
- **Archive write** — minor and steady. Not it.
- **Alerting** — minor. Not it.
- **Archive read (serving saved-searches)** — object-storage GETs, called out as **the single largest line by request count**. This is the one line that grows independently of ingest volume: as the archive accumulates history and as saved-search usage grows, reads fan out over more and more data. This is what's climbing.

And there's a named root cause sitting right on top of it: **page granularity was never tuned since launch.** Pages are small, so every saved-search read shatters into many small object-storage GET requests. Object storage bills largely **per request**, so a query that scans N bytes pays for far more GETs than the bytes require. That multiplier is baked into every read and gets worse as the archive and query load grow — exactly a cost that rises without ingest rising.

## Why tuning page granularity is the highest-leverage lever

Rank the three untried options against two tests — *does it hit the biggest line?* and *does it explain the climb?*

| Lever | Hits the largest bill line? | Explains the volume-decoupled climb? | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase archive page/chunk size** | Yes — directly attacks the #1 line by request count | Yes — read fan-out is what grows without ingest | **Best** |
| Batch dedup comparisons over a wider window | No — targets dedup compute, which is flat with ingest | No | Wrong axis |
| Parallelize dedup across threads | No — and worse: parallelizing single-threaded work cuts **latency**, not total CPU-seconds; total compute *cost* stays roughly the same or rises | No | Not a cost fix at all |

Increasing page granularity is the best lever because it is the only change that satisfies **both** tests: it strikes the single largest line item on the bill *and* it's aimed at the exact mechanism whose cost decoupled from ingest volume. Larger pages mean the same saved-search reads the same bytes in **far fewer, larger GETs** — request count drops close to linearly with the size increase (e.g. 10× larger pages ≈ ~10× fewer read requests for the same query). It's a **configuration/re-chunking change on the columnar archive, not an architecture rewrite**, so it's low-risk and cheap relative to its payoff.

The two dedup levers are tempting because dedup is the *biggest compute number* — but that's a distractor. Dedup compute is flat-with-ingest, so it isn't the source of the increase, and parallelizing it wouldn't lower total cost even if it were. Optimizing the largest *steady* cost is worth doing later; killing the largest *growing* cost is the priority now.

**Validate before/after with the per-stage instrumentation you already have:** the archive-read request-count and object-storage GET spend should fall sharply once pages are enlarged, while ingest metrics stay unchanged — confirming the fan-out was the driver. Pick a page size that balances GET-count reduction against read amplification (larger pages mean each query pulls more bytes per object), then re-chunk existing archive segments so historical saved-searches benefit too, not just new writes.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive's object-storage page/chunk size (re-chunking existing segments) so each saved-search read fans out into far fewer GET requests, collapsing the largest and fastest-growing line on the bill.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The defining clue is that **cost climbed while ingested volume stayed flat**. That immediately rules out any cost that scales with ingest volume — if the driver tracked lines-in, a flat volume would give a flat bill. So the thing that grew must scale with something *other* than ingest volume. The scratch log points to exactly one such line item.

**The dedup step is a distraction here, despite being the biggest compute slice.** It's the majority of the *compute* bill, which is what tempts you to optimize it first — but two things disqualify it as the fix:
- Dedup work is O(lines ingested). With volume flat, its cost is flat. It's a big *level*, not the *climb* we're asked to reverse.
- The two untried dedup ideas don't reduce aggregate cost anyway. **Parallelizing across threads cuts latency, not spend** — you perform the same number of hash comparisons, just concurrently; billed CPU-seconds are unchanged and coordination overhead can make it slightly *worse*. Batching the window is speculative and still aimed at a volume-bound slice that didn't grow.

**The archive read step is the real driver.** Object-storage reads serving saved-search queries are the single largest line by request count, and object-storage GETs are **priced per request**. Crucially, saved-search query load scales with *usage* — more users, dashboards, and saved searches accumulating over the quarter — not with ingest volume. That is precisely a cost that climbs while ingested volume holds flat. And it's amplified by a fixable defect: the archive **page granularity has never been tuned since launch**, so pages are small and every single saved-search read fans out into many small GET requests.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Increase the archive page/chunk granularity — write larger objects to storage — so each saved-search read is satisfied by far fewer, larger GET requests.**

Why this is the best lever:
- **It hits the line item that both grew and dominates.** It's the largest line by request count *and* the one plausibly responsible for the volume-decoupled climb. Nothing else on the bill is both.
- **Per-request pricing makes the payoff near-proportional.** Collapsing N small pages into one larger page cuts the request count for a given saved search by roughly N×. Because request count (not bytes) dominates this line, that translates almost directly into cost reduction — potentially an order of magnitude — while the data served is identical.
- **Lowest cost and risk to implement.** It's a write-path *config/tuning* change, not a re-architecture. It leaves query semantics unchanged, is reversible, and needs no new concurrency machinery.
- **Tune, don't maximize.** Push page size up from "small" to a balanced target and watch for read amplification (over-large pages force each query to read bytes it doesn't need). The sweet spot is the largest page where a typical saved search still reads mostly-useful bytes — but the current direction is unambiguously "too small."

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive object-storage page/chunk size so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer per-request-billed GETs.

## Diagnosis

The bill breakdown and the flat-volume clue point cleanly at one place. Here's the reasoning:

**Where the money actually is, and what grew.** There are two heavy cost centers:
- **Ingest/dedup** — the majority of the *compute* bill (single-threaded hash-and-compare of every line against the recent-window index).
- **Archive reads** serving saved-searches — the single largest line item on the *whole* bill by object-storage GET request count.

The decisive filter is "cost up, ingested volume flat." Dedup work is a function of *incoming lines*. If ingested volume is flat, dedup's workload — and therefore its cost — is essentially flat too. It's big, but it's *steady*; it is not what climbed. So even though dedup dominates compute, it cannot be the thing driving a quarter-over-quarter *increase*, and optimizing it would be chasing a cost that didn't move.

The archive-read line is the one that both **is the largest** and can **grow independently of ingest volume**: it's billed per GET request, and request count is driven by query/read activity and by how many objects each read touches — neither of which is tied to how many logs you ingest. That's the cost/volume decoupling the team observed.

**The root cause of the read cost.** The infra note names it: archive **page granularity has never been tuned since launch — pages are small**, so a single saved-search read *fans out into many small GET requests*. Object storage charges per request, so small pages multiply the request count on every read. This is amplification baked into the storage layout, and it compounds as stored data and saved-search usage grow.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Increase the archive page (chunk) size written to object storage, so each saved-search read resolves in far fewer GET requests.**

Why this beats the alternatives:

- **Magnitude** — it directly attacks the single largest line item on the bill (reads by request count). Coarser pages collapse the GET fan-out roughly in proportion to the size increase (e.g., 8× larger pages ≈ ~⅛ the request count for the same scan), which is a first-order cut, not a trim.
- **It matches the symptom** — read-request billing is exactly the cost that can rise while ingest volume stays flat. Fixing it addresses *what actually grew*.
- **Tractability** — it's a storage-layout/config change plus re-chunking going forward, not a rearchitecture. Low risk, no correctness change to the pipeline's semantics.
- **Durability** — it removes the amplification at the source, so every future read benefits and the line item stops scaling pathologically with usage.

Why the two untried compute ideas are weaker levers:
- **Parallelizing dedup across threads** reduces *latency*, not total core-hours — the same work spread over more threads generally costs the same or more. And it targets a cost that didn't rise (flat volume → flat dedup).
- **Batching dedup over a wider window** changes dedup effectiveness and adds recent-window index cost; again it targets steady compute, not the line item that climbed, and carries correctness/memory tradeoffs.

Both optimize the biggest *compute* slice, but that slice isn't the source of the increase and isn't the biggest *bill* line. The page-size fix is the one change that hits the largest, fastest-growing, volume-decoupled cost at its root.

(One caveat to tune, not a blocker: larger pages mean each GET pulls more bytes, so for highly selective saved-searches you trade request cost for a little more bytes-read. Since the dominant billed dimension here is *request count* and pages are currently undersized from launch, raise page size to right-size it — increase substantially, then verify against read-byte cost rather than maximizing blindly.)

Worth noting: my recall pass primed me toward compute-side suspects (retry reprocessing, per-stage metrics), but the team's data overrides that anchor — the evidence points squarely at read-path GET amplification, not compute.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive object-storage page/chunk size so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer GET requests, cutting the largest (read-request) line on the bill.

## Diagnosis

**The bill grew because of query-driven archive reads, not ingest.** The one symptom that constrains everything is: *cost up sharply, log volume flat.* Any cost that scales with ingested volume — the dedup compute, the archive writes, the parser, the alert windowing — should have stayed roughly flat too. So the growth is coming from a line item that scales with something *other* than log volume. The data points squarely at one: **archive reads serving saved-search queries.** These are the single largest line item on the bill by request count, and they scale with query/analyst activity, which has grown over the quarter — exactly the "cost rose without volume rising" signature.

**Why the reads are so expensive: tiny page granularity.** The archive is columnar-compressed objects in object storage, and the page/chunk size written per object has never been tuned since launch. Object storage bills heavily *per request*. Because pages are small, one saved-search read fans out into many small GET requests — so every query pays a large per-request multiplier. This is a structural amplifier that is entirely independent of how many log lines you ingest.

**The fix: increase the archive page granularity** (write larger objects), and re-pack the existing small pages into coarser ones as a one-time backfill. Each saved-search read then satisfies from far fewer, larger GETs, collapsing the request count multiplicatively on the largest line of the bill. Columnar compression and scan performance are unaffected (larger row-groups typically compress and scan *better*).

## Why this is the highest-leverage lever

- **It hits the largest line on the whole bill,** not the largest slice of a smaller sub-bill. Dedup is the majority of the *compute* portion, but the archive-read request charges are the single biggest line item overall. Attacking the biggest line yields the most reduction per unit of effort.
- **It's root-cause-aligned with the symptom.** Read fan-out scales with query volume, which grew; ingest cost scales with log volume, which didn't. Page granularity is precisely the knob on the line that actually moved.
- **The two ingest "not yet tried" ideas are distractors here:**
  - *Parallelizing dedup across threads* is a **latency/throughput** change, not a **cost** change. The same total lines get hashed and compared, so you pay roughly the same CPU-time (across more cores) — it can even raise the bill. It does nothing for the line that grew.
  - *Batching dedup across a wider window* also targets compute that is proportional to (flat) ingest volume, so it can't explain or reverse the increase — and widening the dedup window risks *more* comparison work, not less.
- **Low risk, high ROI:** it's a configuration/repack change to storage layout, with no change to ingest correctness, dedup semantics, or alerting.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive's object-storage page/chunk granularity (write larger pages and re-pack existing small ones) so each saved-search read fans out into far fewer GET requests, cutting the largest line on the bill.

The decisive clue is the one in the problem statement: **the bill climbed while ingested log volume stayed flat.** That immediately tells you the growth is coming from a cost that is *decoupled from ingest volume*. Line the breakdown up against that filter:

| Step | Cost shape | Scales with… | Could it explain a volume-flat climb? |
|---|---|---|---|
| ingest / dedup | majority of **compute** | every incoming line hashed → **log volume** | No — volume is flat, so this is flat |
| archive write | minor, steady | volume | No |
| **archive read (saved-search)** | **largest line by request count** | **query activity × GET fan-out per query** | **Yes** — independent of ingest volume |
| alerting | minor | volume | No |

Only the **archive read** line is decoupled from ingest volume, and it's the single largest line item by object-storage request count. The infra channel already named the root cause: **archive page granularity was never tuned since launch — pages are small, so one saved-search read fans out into many small object-storage GET requests.** Object storage bills per-GET, so cost tracks *request count*, not bytes. As saved-search usage grows over a quarter (more analysts, dashboards, scheduled queries), request count — and therefore the bill — climbs while ingested volume doesn't move. That is exactly the observed signature.

The fix: **increase the archive page/chunk size written to object storage**, so each saved-search scan reads a few large objects instead of a swarm of tiny ones. Request count falls roughly inversely with page size (≈10× larger pages ≈ ≈10× fewer GETs for the same scan), directly shrinking the largest and fastest-growing line item. Because the archive is already columnar-compressed, larger pages keep analytical scans efficient — you're batching the read, not scanning more data.

## Why this is the best lever — and why the tempting alternative isn't

The seductive miss is the **dedup step**, because it's "the majority of the compute bill." But it fails the leverage test on two counts:

1. **It's not what grew.** Dedup hashes every incoming line, so its cost is a function of log volume — which is flat. A big *level* of cost that isn't *rising* is not the source of the regression. Fixing it wouldn't reverse the climb.
2. **The proposed dedup fixes don't cut cost anyway.** *Parallelizing across threads* reduces wall-clock latency, not billed compute — you pay for the same total CPU work (arguably more vCPU-hours). It's a throughput fix wearing a cost-fix costume. *Batching over a wider window* might catch a few more duplicates, but the only downstream savings would land in the archive-*write* step, which is already "minor, steady."

The page-granularity change wins on every leverage dimension: it targets the **largest** line item, the reduction is **multiplicative** (fewer, larger GETs), it's the **one cost that actually rose** and matches the volume-flat signature, and it's a **low-effort, low-risk config/tuning change** (a chunk-size parameter plus a repack of the archive), not a rewrite. Highest cost × largest achievable cut × best fit to the symptom × least effort — that's the definition of the top lever.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive's object-storage page/chunk granularity so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer, larger GET requests.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The single highest-leverage change: move the retrieval / context-fetch step off the full-priced standard model onto the cheap small model.** The scratch log already ran this experiment and measured a **~14% drop in total per-doc cost** — enough to more than reverse the entire 22% QoQ rise's momentum on the line that's actually causing it.

### Why the cost is rising, and why this is the best lever

The two-step pipeline gives you two candidate levers. Rank them by *unrealized, growing* spend, not by raw size:

**Generation — the larger slice, but the wrong target.** It's *flat* quarter over quarter, so it isn't what's driving the 22% climb. And it's already been squeezed twice: it runs on the **batch-discount API path** and its context length was **capped in the Q2 trim**. Both savings are already baked into today's numbers. Attacking generation means fighting for scraps on a line that (a) isn't growing and (b) has had its two biggest knobs already turned. Low remaining leverage.

**Retrieval — smaller (~40%) but the entire growth term and completely untouched.** It's *the one line still growing*, and it still makes **full-priced calls to the standard model** — no discount path, no cheaper tier. That combination (rising + full-price + un-optimized) is exactly where leverage lives: it's the marginal dollar you haven't spent effort on yet. The model tier is the dominant cost multiplier on that step, and dropping to the small model is the biggest single price cut available to it.

**The experiment settles it empirically.** You don't have to model this — the logged batch already showed **~14% off total per-doc cost** from this one swap. Against a 22% QoQ increase that is the difference between "cost keeps climbing" and "cost is back down and the growing line is neutralized."

### Guardrails before you ship it
- **Confirm downstream quality holds.** The experiment measured *cost*; verify the small model's retrieved context doesn't degrade the generation step's output (the summaries it feeds). The 14% is only a win if quality is unchanged — measure that on the same batch, not just spend.
- **This is orthogonal to your existing per-source context cache** (keyed by document hash). That cache already skips fetches for repeated documents; the fact that retrieval cost is *still growing* suggests your corpus is mostly-unique docs where the cache rarely hits — which is exactly why cutting the *per-call* price is the right move rather than trying to squeeze more cache hits.

RECOMMENDATION: Switch the retrieval / context-fetch step from the full-priced standard model to the cheap small model (validated at ~14% total per-doc cost reduction), after confirming retrieved-context quality holds.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The metrics localize the failure precisely. The split is the whole story:

- Tickets phrased like the article's **originating ticket** → precision@3 **0.95** (near ceiling).
- Tickets phrased **differently** → precision@3 **0.34** — and this bucket is now the **majority** of volume.

So the KB *content* is fine (the topics are covered, and when a query speaks the article's own language, retrieval nails it). The failure is on the **document side of retrieval**: each article is indexed only against its own title/body, which is written in essentially one voice — the phrasing of the single ticket that prompted its creation. The index therefore represents each article in a narrow slice of phrasing-space. Real users describe the same problem many other ways, land in the 0.34 bucket, get no relevant result, and escalate. Deflection falls because the low-precision subset is where the volume now concentrates.

This also explains the *slow slide with a flat article count*: as Deskmate's traffic diversified, more tickets fell into the differently-phrased majority, dragging the aggregate down even though nothing about the articles changed. (Note: my recall pass initially suspected recency-decay and compounding feedback-downweight — the data overturns both; the deficit is a phrasing/representation gap, not a ranking-order problem.)

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Enrich each article's search-index representation with the diverse real ticket phrasings it actually resolves — harvest them from Deskmate's own search + resolution logs and index articles against those, not just their own title/body.**

In effect: index each article against *the many questions it answers*, not against *the one way its author described it*. This is document-side paraphrase expansion (doc2query-style), fed by data Deskmate already logs (query + top-3 results, and deflection outcomes). No new articles need to be written.

## Why it's the best lever

- **It targets the money bucket.** Both the volume *and* the deficit sit in the differently-phrased subset (0.34, majority). The matched subset is already at 0.95 — nothing to win there. Closing the 0.34 gap is mathematically where aggregate precision@3 and deflection move the most.
- **It attacks the identified mechanism directly.** The 0.95-vs-0.34 split *is* a document-representation vocabulary gap. Widening what each article can be found by is the mechanism-matched fix.
- **Recency re-weighting (the tempting "not yet tried" lever) targets the wrong thing.** The 0.95 subset proves the ranker already surfaces the *right* article when phrasing matches — so ordering isn't the bottleneck; matching is. Re-ranking by edit date reshuffles results without making a differently-phrased query match its article, and risks demoting correct-but-stable articles. Untried ≠ high-leverage.
- **Synonym expansion is empirically spent.** It shipped last quarter and deflection *kept sliding through it* — word-level swaps (invoice→bill) don't bridge structural/conceptual paraphrase. More of it is low expected value.
- **Swapping the embedding model is higher cost, higher risk, lower leverage.** Even a stronger model still only encodes each article's narrow self-description — garbage-in on the document side. Expansion helps under *any* model, and doesn't spend the low-latency budget the compact model was chosen for.

RECOMMENDATION: Index each KB article against the diverse real ticket phrasings it resolves (mined from Deskmate's search/resolution logs), instead of only the article's own title/body.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The data redirects my earlier hypothesis. This is **not** a leak in conflict-resolution metadata or the gRPC stream — it's the per-shard sync-state buffers accumulating in a **single, long-lived, shared address space** that the process can never hand back to the OS. Two facts in the scratch log carry the whole diagnosis:

**1. Why it's killed *more often* over a month (the trend).** Each shard's buffer "grows slowly over its lifetime." Shard count is flat, but per-shard resident size creeps up, so the daily bulk-resync peak — when every buffer expands at once — creeps higher week over week until it crosses the cgroup limit routinely. The workload isn't growing; the resident high-water mark is.

**2. Why every in-process fix has failed (the decisive signal).** You have already run the two obvious levers, and *both failed for the same reason*:
- v2.3 explicit post-resync release → recovered only ~10%
- v2.2 shared cross-shard buffer pool → negligible win

In both cases allocator **fragmentation in the long-lived process kept freed pages resident**. That is the key invariant: in a long-lived process, RSS is floored by the allocation high-water mark. Freeing memory logically does not return it to the OS once the heap is fragmented. So *any* fix that stays inside the single address space is capped at roughly that ~10% ceiling — you've measured it twice.

## The highest-leverage lever

**Per-shard subprocess isolation** — run each shard's sync in its own spawned process.

It is the best lever precisely because it's the **only** option on the table that changes the variable that neutralized both prior attempts. The failed fixes fought fragmentation *within* a permanent address space; the one reliable way to reclaim a fragmented long-lived heap is to **end the process** — `exit()` unmaps the entire address space, returning 100% (grown live buffers *and* fragmented free space) instead of the ~10% an in-process release can reach. It attacks the root — the shared, never-terminating address space — rather than the symptom. It simultaneously kills the two mechanisms driving the OOMs: the per-lifetime buffer creep (reset at each process's exit) and the synchronized peak (isolated address spaces reclaim independently instead of summing in one RSS number against one cgroup limit).

## Why not the alternatives

- **Another in-process release / allocator tuning (jemalloc arenas, `malloc_trim`, etc.):** v2.2 and v2.3 already establish the ceiling here is ~10%. More effort on this branch buys almost nothing — it's the branch the evidence rules out.
- **Stagger the bulk-resync window:** flattens the synchronized peak, but returns *zero* memory and does nothing about the lifetime creep or fragmentation. Baseline RSS keeps climbing and re-crosses the limit — a reprieve, not a fix.
- **Bound/cap buffer growth:** addresses only the growth term; the already-grown, fragmented, unreturnable resident set stays, and much of the 40–60MB is live working set during resync anyway.
- **Raise the cgroup limit:** defers the kills, doesn't fix them; the creep resumes crossing whatever the new limit is.

Worth flagging honestly: isolation isn't free — IPC/serialization overhead, per-process supervision, and you lose any genuine cross-shard in-process sharing. But those are cost-of-adoption questions, not leverage questions. On leverage, it's the single change that removes the exact constraint your last two fixes died on.

RECOMMENDATION: Move each shard's sync into its own isolated subprocess so the OS fully reclaims its address space (buffers + fragmentation) on process exit, instead of freeing memory inside the shared long-lived daemon process.

## Diagnosis

The dominant cost term and the growing term are two different things, and the lever lives in the growing one.

**Follow the money against what's already optimized:**

| Stage | Share of per-doc cost | Trend | Optimization headroom |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generation | larger slice | **flat** QoQ | ~none — already on batch-discount path + context capped in Q2 |
| Retrieval / context-fetch | ~40% | **still growing** | full — still full-priced calls to the standard model |

The 22% QoQ rise is not coming from generation. Generation is the bigger slice, so it's the instinctive target — but it's **flat**, and it's flat *because it has already been squeezed twice* (batch-discount API since spring, prompt/context capped in the Q2 trim). There's little left to recover there, and any further cut fights diminishing returns.

Retrieval is the opposite profile: it is **the only line still growing** *and* it is **completely unoptimized** — still making full-priced calls to the standard model while generation moved off that path months ago. That combination — it's the marginal growth driver *and* it carries the most unclaimed headroom — is what makes it the highest-leverage point rather than just a cheaper-looking one.

**The experiment already proves the lever works.** Swapping retrieval onto the cheap small model measured **~14% off total per-doc cost** in one batch. That figure is internally consistent: retrieval is ~40% of cost, so a 14% total drop means the retrieval slice fell ~35% of itself — exactly the shape of a standard-model → small-model price move. A validated ~14% total reduction is the single biggest confirmed win on the table, and it directly attacks the line that's causing the increase.

So the best lever is not the biggest stage — it's the stage that is **both the growth driver and the last one still paying full price**, with an experiment already confirming the payoff.

## Diagnosis

The scratch log contains a clean natural experiment that pins the cause precisely:

- Tickets **worded like the article's originating ticket** → precision@3 ≈ **0.95**
- Tickets **worded differently** → precision@3 ≈ **0.34** — and this group is now the **majority** of volume

Same articles, same index, same ranker — the only variable is whether the incoming wording matches the wording that happened to spawn the article. So the retrieval machinery is **not** broken: when the demand-side language matches the indexed language, it finds the right article nearly every time. The correct articles exist and are retrievable.

The failure is a **vocabulary mismatch**. Per the architecture, the index matches on **each article's own title/body** — and articles are authored in response to one originating ticket, so they carry that single ticket's phrasing (often support-agent / cause-and-fix language). Users who describe the same problem in different words (symptoms, lay terms, their own product context) fall into the 0.34 bucket.

**Why it's falling with a fixed corpus:** the article count is constant, but the *query mix* is drifting. Differently-phrased tickets are now the majority and their share is growing (broader/newer user base). Each such ticket lands in the 0.34 regime, so the blended precision@3 (0.71→0.58) and deflection (54%→41%) slide even though nothing about the KB changed. The cause is on the demand side, not the supply side.

## Why the alternatives are not the lever

- **Recency reweighting** (the log's untried idea): zero supporting evidence. The precision split is by *phrasing*, not article *age* — the right article is already the correct, retrievable one; it's simply not matched. Reweighting recency reorders results that were never surfaced for these queries; it can't rescue the 0.34 subset. It's a distractor. *(This overrides my recall-stage prior, which flagged the recency blend and the feedback-downweight ratchet as suspects — the data cleanly attributes the loss to phrasing, not age or monotonic suppression, so both priors yield.)*
- **Synonym/paraphrase expansion**: already shipped last quarter, and deflection kept sliding *through* it. Hand-curated synonyms don't cover the long tail of how users actually phrase problems. Doubling down repeats a known failure.
- **A bigger embedding model**: would help paraphrase matching generically, but it's a heavy, blunt change that still matches against the article's *authored* words — it hopes a model bridges a gap we can close directly with observed data. The already-failed expansion pass shows generic phrasing-widening isn't sufficient.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Index each article against the real, varied user phrasings of the problem it solves — not just its own title/body.** Concretely: mine the search/ticket logs (which already capture query→result pairs) for the multiple ways users describe each article's issue, and fold those phrasings into the article's indexed representation (query-aligned / "index the demand side"); seed synthetic paraphrase variants for low-traffic articles that lack log coverage.

Why it's the best lever: it targets the failure that is simultaneously the **largest** (majority of volume) and the **cause of the downward trend** (that majority is growing). The 0.95-vs-0.34 split proves the ranker and articles are sound and that closing the wording gap converts the failing majority into the near-perfect regime — the highest-yield move available. Every alternative is already-tried, evidence-free, or a costlier proxy for this same idea. Use the existing top-3 relevance logs to measure lift on the differently-phrased subset.

RECOMMENDATION: Index each KB article against the actual, varied user query/ticket phrasings that resolve to it (enrich the article's indexed representation with demand-side language), instead of matching only on the article's own title/body.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The scratch log contains a natural experiment that isolates the cause. Holding the article set, the embedding model, and the ranker constant, precision@3 swings from **0.95 to 0.34** based on a single variable: whether the incoming ticket is phrased like the ticket that *originally prompted the article's creation*.

That is a **vocabulary-mismatch problem, not a ranking problem.** The index "matches on each article's own title/body content" — i.e., articles are searchable in the *author's* language. When a user happens to phrase their ticket the way the originating ticket was phrased, the match is near-perfect (0.95). When they phrase it differently — which is now **the majority of volume** — the relevant article often isn't retrieved into the top-3 at all (0.34).

**Why it's falling over weeks with a static article count:** the articles are frozen in the vocabulary of the tickets that created them, while incoming ticket phrasing keeps drifting and diversifying. The differently-phrased subset grows month over month, dragging the blended average (and deflection) down. Back-solving the two subsets against the 0.58 overall puts that failing subset at ~60% of volume and rising — so the decline is structural, not noise.

## Why the other levers lose

- **Recency re-weighting (untried):** reorders results the search *already retrieved*. But the failure mode is that the right article is **not retrieved at all** for differently-phrased queries (0.34). Recency can't surface what the match layer never returned. Worse, the article count is static — articles aren't being edited — so an edit-recency term mostly injects noise. It targets the wrong axis (ordering, not retrieval) and would move nothing.
- **Synonym/paraphrase expansion (already shipped, failed):** this was the query-side attempt to bridge phrasing, and "deflection kept sliding through it." Hand-curated synonym lists can't scale to the open-ended diversity of real user phrasing. Proven ineffective on exactly this problem.
- **Bigger embedding model:** plausible but expensive and uncertain — and the natural experiment already controls for the model. The *same* compact model scores 0.95 when phrasing aligns. The binding constraint isn't the matcher's quality; it's that the text being matched against is in the wrong vocabulary.

## The highest-leverage fix

Index articles against the language **users actually type**, not the language authors wrote. Concretely: expand each article's searchable representation to include the text of the real tickets it resolves — starting with its **originating ticket**, and continuously feeding in tickets it later deflects (the search logs already capture query→result pairs, so the raw material exists).

This is the best lever for three reasons:
1. **It hits the majority of volume where the loss lives.** It moves the ~60% differently-phrased subset from 0.34 toward the 0.95 the aligned subset already demonstrates is achievable — the largest recoverable gain by far. No other lever touches that subset.
2. **It uses real observed phrasing instead of guessed phrasing.** Query-side synonyms must anticipate every paraphrase (and already failed); document expansion draws from the actual distribution of user language you're trying to match.
3. **It's self-healing against the drift that caused the decline.** As phrasing keeps evolving, an index fed by real deflected tickets keeps absorbing new vocabulary automatically, instead of re-freezing in author language and decaying again.

RECOMMENDATION: Expand each KB article's search index representation with the wording of the real tickets it resolves (starting with its originating ticket, continuously augmented from deflected-ticket logs), so articles are matched against actual user phrasing rather than only their own author-written title/body.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The month-long trend has exactly one explanation that fits the data: the per-shard sync-state buffer grows monotonically over a shard's lifetime.** Shard count is flat, so that per-shard growth is the *only* variable that moves. As daemon/shard uptime accumulates week over week, each buffer gets larger, the synchronized bulk-resync spike lands on a higher baseline, and peak RSS crosses the cgroup limit more often. Nothing else in the log trends with time; this does.

So the root cause is **unbounded buffer growth**, and fragmentation is a *downstream symptom* of it, not the disease.

## Why subprocess isolation is the wrong lever

The whiteboard proposal treats fragmentation as root cause. Four problems:

1. **It reclaims only at process exit — but the limit is crossed *during* the simultaneous-resync window, when nothing is exiting.** Every shard's buffer still grows at once, now spread across N address spaces. Subprocess isolation does not lower the peak; it only reclaims later, at exit.

2. **It probably raises total RSS.** N separate processes means per-process runtime/stack/heap-arena overhead ×N and loss of shared pages — added memory precisely when you're trying to shed it.

3. **If the growth is live retained state, it's a treadmill.** The tell is v2.3: an explicit release freed only ~10%. The team read the other 90% as fragmentation, but "only 10% was freeable" is *equally* consistent with 90% being genuinely live, growing buffer content. If that's true, a subprocess reclaims at exit and then the buffer refills and re-crosses the limit before the next exit. Both prior fixes (v2.2 pool, v2.3 release) failed for the same reason a subprocess would: they tried to reclaim memory that is still live.

4. **It's the most expensive option** — moving shards cross-process complicates the persistent gRPC stream transport, per-field last-writer-wins conflict resolution, and the 30s checkpoint coordination — to treat a symptom.

## The highest-leverage fix

**Bound the per-shard sync-state buffer by truncating it up to the last checkpointed sync offset**, so buffer size tracks the ~30s checkpoint interval instead of growing over the shard's whole lifetime.

Each shard already checkpoints its sync offset to disk every 30 seconds. Any buffered state older than the checkpointed offset is already durable and safe to drop. A buffer that "grows slowly over a shard's lifetime" is the signature of that truncation being absent or incomplete — the buffer is retaining history that's already been checkpointed. This also explains why v2.3 only recovered 10%: it released transient post-resync scratch, not the accumulated pre-checkpoint history that is the actual bulk.

Why it's the best lever:

- **It targets the one variable that changed** (buffer size growing with uptime), so it directly reverses the month-long trend instead of masking it.
- **It's upstream of every symptom.** Smaller buffers mean a lower baseline for all shards → the synchronized resync spike fits under the limit; *and* far less allocation churn → less fragmentation to begin with. One fix collapses both the peak and the fragmentation problems that the two prior attempts and the subprocess proposal each chase separately.
- **It's robust to the diagnostic uncertainty.** If the 90% is live retention, bounding is the *only* thing that works. If it's genuinely fragmentation, bounding still cuts allocation volume and shrinks the fragmented footprint. Subprocess isolation only wins in the pure-fragmentation world and loses in the retention world — bounding wins in both.
- **It's cheap and correctness-preserving** — a truncation tied to an offset the shard already persists, not a rearchitecture.

One measurement will confirm the mechanism before you build it: a heap profile of a long-lived shard to verify the unfreeable 90% is live buffer content retaining already-checkpointed offsets (→ this fix) rather than dead fragmented pages (→ an allocator-level `malloc_trim`/arena-reset, still far cheaper than subprocessing). Either way, subprocess isolation is not the lever.

RECOMMENDATION: Bound each shard's sync-state buffer by truncating it up to the last checkpointed sync offset, so buffer size stays bounded by the checkpoint interval instead of growing over the shard's lifetime.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The symptom — RSS climbing month over month while shard count stays flat — points to a **monotonic ratchet**, not a capacity shortfall. The scratch log tells me exactly where the ratchet lives, and, crucially, gives me two independent experiments that already ruled out the obvious levers.

**The binding constraint is the single long-lived shared address space.** Every shard runs in one process. In that process, allocator fragmentation means freed memory is never returned to the OS — so once a page is dirtied it stays resident for the life of the daemon. Buffers grow slowly over each shard's lifetime, resync churns the heap, and because shards are long-lived and the process never dies, RSS only ever goes up. It finally tips over the cgroup limit at the daily bulk-resync peak, when every shard's working set inflates at once. That's why the kills got *more frequent over a month* with no new shards: the process has simply been up long enough to accumulate more un-returnable pages.

**The two prior fixes are the decisive evidence.** Both v2.3 (explicit post-resync release) and v2.2 (shared cross-shard pool) attacked the problem *from inside the process* — they freed memory in user space. Both were defeated by the same thing: fragmentation kept ~90% of the "freed" pages resident. Two independent attempts failing for one identical reason is a strong empirical result: **you cannot reclaim memory inside this long-lived shared address space.** Any lever that keeps all shards in one durable process is fighting a battle those two experiments already lost.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Per-shard subprocess isolation** — run each shard's sync in its own spawned process.

It is the best lever because it is the *only* proposed change that attacks the binding constraint instead of fighting fragmentation from inside it. Process exit is the one reclamation path fragmentation cannot defeat: when the address space is destroyed, every fragmented, un-returnable page goes back to the OS unconditionally. Recycling a shard's process periodically (e.g., after each bulk-resync) resets *both* contributors to the ratchet at once — the slow live-buffer growth **and** the fragmentation debris — which no in-process fix can do. It also converts the bulk-resync peak from one giant shared spike into N independent, separately-reclaimable footprints, so a single misbehaving shard can no longer drag the whole daemon over the limit.

**Why the alternatives are weaker:**
- *Raise the cgroup limit* — doesn't stop the ratchet; the OOM just returns later at a higher ceiling.
- *Cap/free buffers in-process* — this is exactly what v2.3 did; fragmentation kept the pages, ~10% recovered. Proven insufficient.
- *Shared buffer pool* — v2.2; same fragmentation wall, negligible win.
- *Restart the whole daemon* — does destroy the address space, but takes **every** shard down at once; subprocess isolation gets the same OS-level reclaim per shard without a global outage.
- *Swap the allocator (jemalloc/tcmalloc with page purging, or `malloc_trim`)* — the one cheaper probe worth running first to see if better decay returns more than glibc's 10%; but it's uncertain and still fights fragmentation inside a durable process rather than eliminating it. The data suggests the fragmentation is intractable in-process, which is what makes isolation the definitive rather than speculative fix.

The lever isn't "reduce memory" — it's "stop the address space from being immortal." Subprocess isolation is the only option on the table that does that.

RECOMMENDATION: Move each shard's sync into its own recycled subprocess (per-shard process isolation) so the OS reclaims all fragmented memory at process exit.

## Diagnosis

The scratch log contains one dominant signal that overrides everything else:

- Tickets phrased like an article's **originating ticket** → precision@3 ≈ **0.95**
- Tickets phrased **differently** from any originating ticket → precision@3 ≈ **0.34**
- The differently-phrased bucket is **the majority of current volume** — and growing

Everything else is explained by this split. The index "matches on each article's own title/body content" — i.e. articles are stored in *answer voice* (symptom/cause/fix), while incoming tickets are in *problem voice*. Retrieval only works when a ticket happens to echo the phrasing that seeded the article. It's a **query–document vocabulary/representation mismatch**, not a coverage gap.

This also explains *why deflection slides over time with a static article count*: the KB's matchable surface (originating-ticket-like phrasings) is fixed, but the live query distribution keeps drifting toward novel phrasings. Week over week, a larger share of tickets falls into the 0.34 bucket, so aggregate precision@3 (0.71→0.58) and deflection (54%→41%) fall mechanically — no content change required. "Article count unchanged" was always a red herring; coverage was never the binding constraint.

## The highest-leverage fix

**Change what the index matches on: represent each article by the *tickets/questions it answers*, not (only) its own answer-body text.** Concretely — attach each article's originating ticket plus its known resolved-ticket phrasings (and/or generated question paraphrases) and index/embed *those*, so retrieval becomes ticket-to-ticket instead of ticket-to-answer.

## Why this is the best lever

- **It's the only fix aimed at the axis the data isolates.** The failure is cleanly partitioned by *phrasing alignment*. This change converts the majority 0.34 bucket toward the 0.95 regime by making every article matchable in problem voice.
- **Its ceiling is measured, not hypothetical.** The 0.95 subset is a live proof: same embedder, same articles — when matching happens on ticket-style phrasing, retrieval is near-perfect. The fix simply extends that condition to the majority of tickets.
- **It attacks the trend, not just the level.** Because it fixes the growing majority bucket, it arrests the ongoing slide rather than nudging the current number.

By contrast, the other levers target axes the data rules out or has already falsified:

- **Recency re-weighting (the untried, tempting one):** orthogonal. It only *reorders* results; it can't retrieve an article that scores 0.34 as relevant in the first place, and it risks demoting stale-but-correct answers. The data partitions quality by phrasing, not by freshness — so recency addresses a dimension that isn't the problem.
- **More synonym/paraphrase expansion:** already shipped last quarter, and deflection *kept sliding through it*. Word-level substitution ("invoice"→"bill") is empirically insufficient for the structural problem-voice/answer-voice gap. Proven low-leverage.
- **Bigger embedding model:** fixes the *encoder* when the problem is *what's being encoded*. The 0.95-vs-0.34 split shows the article text itself is the wrong thing to match against; upgrading the model on the wrong input yields far less than fixing the representation.

(This also updates my pre-data hypothesis: I'd suspected the feedback-downweight ratchet, but that would degrade specific topic clusters — the data instead shows a clean split by phrasing, which the ratchet can't explain. The evidence points to representation, so I'm following it.)

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The failure is a **query–document vocabulary gap**, and the scratch log localizes it precisely.

The two-way split is the whole story:
- Tickets worded like an article's **originating ticket** → precision@3 **0.95**.
- Tickets worded **differently** → precision@3 **0.34** — and that subset is now the **majority** of volume.

So coverage isn't the problem (the KB *contains* the right article — it's found 0.95 of the time when the words line up), and article count being flat is a red herring. What's happening is that each article is indexed on **its own authored title/body**, which is frozen in the vocabulary of the one ticket that spawned it. Retrieval therefore rewards surface-wording overlap. As Deskmate's incoming traffic drifts toward phrasings that don't echo those originating tickets, a growing share of tickets fall into the 0.34 bucket. That's exactly why deflection **slides week over week with a constant corpus**: the mismatched-phrasing share keeps rising.

## Why the other levers are wrong or already spent

- **Synonym/paraphrase expansion** already shipped last quarter and deflection kept sliding *through it*. Token-level bridging (`invoice`→`bill`) is proven insufficient — the mismatch is at the level of whole-phrasing meaning, not individual word swaps.
- **Recency re-weighting** is the tempting "not yet tried" option, but it's orthogonal to the failing dimension. The 0.34 tickets don't fail because the right article ranks *just below* a fresher one — they fail because the article isn't semantically matched at all. Re-ordering by edit-recency does nothing for a paraphrased query and risks surfacing recently-touched-but-irrelevant articles. (This also corrects the recency hypothesis I floated after recall — the data contradicts it.)
- **Adding articles** — count is flat and irrelevant; the content already exists.

## The single highest-leverage fix

The root cause is that the **compact, latency-optimized sentence-embedding model** (chosen deliberately as a quality-for-speed tradeoff) is too weak to map differently-worded queries and articles into the same meaning space. Dense retrieval's entire purpose is paraphrase robustness; a 0.34 on paraphrased queries means the current embeddings are effectively acting lexical, not semantic. That one component is the architectural bottleneck sitting under the majority subset.

**Upgrade the embedding model to a stronger semantic model and re-embed the index.** This is the best lever because:

1. **It attacks the proven root cause** — semantic (not surface) matching — which is the exact axis the diagnostic split isolates.
2. **Widest reach** — it lifts the *majority, growing* 0.34 subset in one change, rather than nibbling per-article.
3. **It generalizes to unseen phrasings.** The mismatched-phrasing share is expanding over time (new users, drifting language). A better embedding model handles *tomorrow's* novel wordings; log-based tricks only chase yesterday's. This is what makes it higher-leverage than doc-side expansion from query logs — expansion covers phrasings you've already seen; a stronger model covers the ones you haven't.
4. **The cheaper family is already ruled out** — token-level synonym expansion was tried and failed, which is direct evidence that the remaining path is genuine semantic depth, i.e. the model itself.

Guard the tradeoff it was originally chosen against: benchmark the new model's added query latency against the per-session query cache and pick a model that clears your latency budget — but the precision collapse on the majority subset dwarfs a modest latency cost.

RECOMMENDATION: Replace Deskmate's compact latency-optimized sentence-embedding model with a stronger semantic embedding model and re-embed the KB index, so retrieval matches on meaning rather than shared wording.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The scratch log resolves the earlier ambiguity: this is not a leak in the "references pile up" sense and it is not driven by shard count. It's a **fragmentation ratchet in a single long-lived address space**, and the highest-leverage fix is the whiteboard proposal — **per-shard subprocess isolation with recycling**. Here's the reasoning chain and why it beats the alternatives.

**What the data actually shows**

1. **Two independent growth drivers, one address space.** Each shard's sync-state buffer is 40–60MB and *creeps up over the shard's lifetime*; separately, all buffers spike together during the daily bulk-resync. The month-over-month worsening at flat shard count is the lifetime creep plus a ratchet — the process never gives memory back between cycles, so baseline RSS climbs until the resync spike tips it over the cgroup limit.

2. **The decisive clue is v2.3.** The post-resync release freed only ~10%. That means ~90% of the memory the daemon *logically* frees stays resident — so a large fraction of RSS is fragmentation-held freed pages, not live working set. The daemon holds far more RSS than its live data. That is the lever: recover the non-live resident memory and the peak fits.

3. **Two cheaper fixes already failed for the *same* reason.** v2.2 (shared cross-shard pool) and v2.3 (explicit release) both tried to return memory *within one persistent address space*, and both were defeated by the same mechanism: fragmentation. A single live object sprinkled across a page pins the whole page; in a long-lived heap with interleaved variable-size buffers, no in-process reclaim — and likely no mere allocator swap — reliably returns those pages.

**Why subprocess isolation is the best lever**

It's the *only* proposal that stops fighting fragmentation and instead removes the condition that produces it — the shared, persistent address space. When a shard's subprocess exits, the OS reclaims 100% of its pages (live *and* fragmented) unconditionally; the allocator's cooperation is no longer required. Recycle the subprocess each cycle (e.g., after its resync) and you neutralize *both* growth drivers at once:

- **Lifetime creep** can't accumulate — a short-lived recycled process has no long "lifetime" to creep over.
- **Fragmentation ratchet** can't accumulate — the heap is torn down and rebuilt fresh each cycle, so baseline RSS resets instead of climbing.

That "kills both drivers with one change" property is what makes it the single highest-leverage fix rather than needing a bundle of them. It also adds fault isolation as a free byproduct (one shard's blowup no longer OOMs its neighbors and the whole daemon).

**Why the alternatives lose**

- **Raise the cgroup limit** — delays only; the ratchet keeps climbing and re-crosses. Not a fix.
- **Swap the allocator (jemalloc/tcmalloc, aggressive page return)** — the strongest competitor and worth trying if isolation is too costly, but it's a *bet that fragmentation is lazy-page-return, not structural*. The two failed in-process attempts are evidence it's structural (interleaved live buffers pinning pages), which no allocator fully escapes. Subprocess teardown is robust to whatever the fragmentation cause is; the allocator swap is not.
- **Stagger the bulk-resync so buffers don't all peak together** — flattens the simultaneity spike but leaves the ratchet climbing; over more days it re-crosses. Reshapes the symptom, doesn't restore headroom.
- **Shrink/cap the per-shard buffer** — attacks live size, but the v2.3 evidence says most of the over-limit RSS is *non-live fragmented* memory, so this leaves the dominant term on the table.

**One caveat to make it actually work:** the win comes from *recycling* — a subprocess that is spawned once and never exits fragments exactly as badly as today. The design must tear down and respawn each shard's process on a cadence (post-resync is the natural trigger) so the OS reclaim actually fires.

RECOMMENDATION: Move each shard's sync into its own recycled subprocess (per-shard subprocess isolation, respawned each resync cycle) so the OS reclaims the entire address space on exit — defeating the allocator fragmentation that made the v2.2 pool and v2.3 release ineffective.
recommendation: Move each shard's sync into its own recycled subprocess (per-shard subprocess isolation, respawned each resync cycle) so the OS reclaims the entire address space on exit — defeating the allocator fragmentation that made the v2.2 pool and v2.3 release ineffective.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

Start from the one fact the trend has to be consistent with: **shard count is flat, but OOM kills are rising month over month.** If the number of shards didn't change, the only variable that can explain "worse over time" is the *size of each shard's buffer* — and the log states exactly that: **each sync-state buffer grows slowly over a shard's lifetime.** As the daemon and its shards age, every buffer is individually larger today than a month ago, so the daily bulk-resync peak — the moment every buffer balloons at once — now clears the cgroup limit where it used to fit under it.

So the driver is **unbounded per-shard buffer growth**, and fragmentation is a *downstream symptom*, not the cause. Fragmentation only matters for memory you've freed; it's the reason freeing didn't help (v2.3) and pooling didn't help (v2.2). But you only have fragmented, un-returnable pages *because* the buffers grow and churn in the first place. Both prior fixes, and the subprocess proposal, aim at "how do we give freed memory back to the OS" — the wrong end of the causal chain.

## Why subprocess isolation is not the best lever

It's the flashiest proposal on the board, but it treats the symptom at the highest possible cost:

- **It doesn't stop the growth.** The OS only reclaims "at process exit." A long-lived shard subprocess accumulates the exact same lifetime growth and fragmentation inside its own address space — the win only materializes if you *also* recycle those processes. The real mechanism doing the work is recycling, not isolation.
- **It doesn't lower the peak where the OOM happens.** The kill occurs at the simultaneous bulk-resync peak. N subprocesses resyncing at once sum to the same peak RSS; isolation only cleans up *between* resyncs.
- **It can raise total memory.** Each subprocess re-pays a runtime/heap baseline and loses the shared address space — the opposite of what the v2.2 shared-pool prototype was reaching for. Plus IPC, supervision, and deploy complexity.

High cost, high risk, and it leaves the actual driver in place.

## Highest-leverage fix

**Bound the per-shard sync-state buffer by evicting everything at or below each shard's last checkpointed sync offset.** Each shard already checkpoints its sync offset to disk every 30s, so any buffered state below that durable offset is redundant and safe to drop — a correctness-preserving cap. This is the best lever because it's the only one that acts on the variable actually driving the regression:

1. **It reverses the trend at its source.** Buffers stop growing with age, so the month-over-month creep — the whole reason kills are increasing — flattens. Shard count being stable means a bounded per-shard footprint yields a bounded total.
2. **It lowers *both* baseline and peak.** Smaller steady-state buffers shrink the coincident bulk-resync spike, moving peak RSS back under the limit — exactly where the OOM fires.
3. **It dissolves the fragmentation problem instead of fighting it.** Fragmentation scales with how much you allocate-and-free; a bounded, steady-state working set churns far less, so the very obstacle that defeated v2.2 and v2.3 largely disappears. You avoid creating the problem rather than paying a re-architecture to escape it.
4. **It's cheap and low-risk** relative to per-shard subprocess isolation — a trimming rule keyed off state the system already tracks, no IPC, no new failure modes, no per-process memory tax.

If growth still needs a backstop after that, a cheap periodic shard/daemon recycle captures ~all of subprocess isolation's reclamation benefit without the architectural cost — but the buffer-trim is the primary lever.

RECOMMENDATION: Cap each shard's in-memory sync-state buffer by evicting entries at or below its last checkpointed sync offset, stopping the unbounded lifetime growth that drives the rising OOM kills.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The cost driver is the integration suite's retry multiplier — and it's structurally fixed.**

The bill grows faster than commit volume because per-commit cost isn't `commits × work`; it's `commits × (integration-suite minutes) × 2.6 attempts × blocking-lane rate`. The integration suite is the largest compute slice, and every merge pays for ~2.6 full runs of it because the vendored conformance suite is flaky and the runner is all-or-nothing. That 2.6× amplifier riding on the largest slice is why spend outruns commits.

**Now walk the levers and watch all but one die against the constraints:**

| Lever | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Fix the flakiness | **Blocked** — the conformance suite is vendored and pinned upstream; you can't patch it. |
| Quarantine the flaky subset / partial rerun | **Blocked** — no per-test rerun API (all-or-nothing runner), and certification requires a *fresh full-suite pass*, so partial reruns don't count. |
| Run nightly / sample merges (cut frequency) | **Blocked** — compliance requires a certified full-suite pass recorded for *every* merged change. |
| More caching | **Exhausted** — fixture caching shipped in Q1; rebuild is already only ~6% of runtime, the rest is the irreducible conformance tests. |
| Move to the preemptible background lane | **Open** — and it's the only one left standing. |

So the compute-**minutes** are locked: you can't cut the flakiness, the retry count, the frequency, or the remaining runtime. The only movable quantity left is the **price per minute**.

**Why the preemptible lane is the best — indeed the only high-leverage — lever:**

1. **It hits the biggest slice.** The integration suite dominates the bill, so a rate cut there moves the total more than anything acting on unit shards (a stable minority) ever could.
2. **It compounds with the multiplier you can't remove.** At ~40% of the blocking rate, it's a ~60% cost cut, and it applies to *all 2.6 attempts*. The very multiplier that makes each commit expensive now gets billed at 40% — you turn the amplifier's downside into leverage instead of fighting it.
3. **The constraints that kill every other option don't touch this one.** The policy requires a certified full-suite pass *recorded* per merge, but explicitly **does not require the run to block the merge** — a non-blocking background run still satisfies it. And **nothing downstream gates on the suite finishing**, so preemption's interruptions and added latency are free; you're spending a resource (wall-clock/completion latency) you don't actually need in exchange for the one you're overpaying for (blocking-lane compute).

The other levers aren't just weaker — they're foreclosed by the flakiness, the runner, and the compliance policy. Preemptible capacity is the single fix that survives all three constraints while acting on the largest, most-amplified slice of the bill.

*(One flag for the rollout, not a change to the recommendation: preemptible interruptions mean occasional mid-run kills that re-execute whole, adding some re-run minutes — but even generous preemption waste is dwarfed by the 60% rate cut, and since nothing gates on completion the retries are harmless.)*

RECOMMENDATION: Move the integration suite off the blocking lane onto the preemptible background lane, keeping the certified full-suite pass recorded per merge (non-blocking).

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The compute bill has decoupled from commit volume because the cost is dominated by a **per-run multiplier**, not by how many runs there are. Walking the data against Conveyor's architecture:

**Where the money is:** The integration suite is the single largest compute-minutes slice, and it runs an average of **2.6 full-suite attempts per run** before it passes. That ~2.6× multiplier is the decoupling: each commit doesn't buy one suite execution, it buys ~2.6, and it applies to the most expensive slice. Every extra commit multiplies against an already-inflated per-run cost, so spend grows faster than commits.

**Why you can't cut the *amount* of that compute — every direct path is walled off:**
- **Fix the flakiness?** The failures are in the vendored conformance suite, pinned upstream, unpatchable.
- **Rerun only the failed tests?** The runner is all-or-nothing — no per-test rerun API, so a failure re-executes the whole suite.
- **Quarantine the flaky subset?** The certification report requires a fresh *full-suite* pass on every run; partial reruns don't count.
- **Run nightly / sample merges?** The compliance policy requires a certified full-suite pass recorded for *every merged change*.
- **Cache the fixture build?** Already shipped in Q1 — the rebuild is now only ~6% of runtime; the rest is the conformance tests themselves. Nothing left there.
- **Unit shards / artifact retention?** Shards are a stable minority slice; retention is storage, not compute. Red herrings.

So the 2.6× multiplier is genuinely fixed — you *must* pay for ~2.6 full-suite executions per merged change to get a certified pass. You cannot reduce the number of minutes.

## The one lever that actually moves

Since you can't make those minutes **fewer**, make them **cheaper**. The one hidden degree of freedom in the constraint set is the policy's exact wording: it requires a certified full-suite pass to be *recorded* for every merged change — it does **not** require the run to block the merge. And nothing downstream gates on the suite finishing.

That means the entire suite can leave the blocking lane for the **preemptible background lane at ~40% of the rate** — a **~60% cost reduction on the single largest, most inflated slice**, applied to all of its minutes including the 2.6× retries. No other available action touches the biggest slice; every one that would is blocked by the vendored/compliance walls. This is the highest-leverage fix precisely *because* it's the only lever that isn't blocked and it hits the largest slice.

**Why the obvious objection doesn't sink it:** preemptible capacity can be reclaimed mid-run, and with an all-or-nothing runner a preempted execution restarts from scratch — so preemption inflates executions on top of the 2.6× multiplier. But run the break-even: cost is `0.4 × 2.6/(1−p)` vs. baseline `1.0 × 2.6`, which only loses money once the preemption rate **p exceeds 60%**. Real preemptible preemption rates sit far below that, so the lever is robustly net-positive with wide margin. And because nothing gates on completion, the added latency from restarts is harmless to developer flow.

**Two implementation guardrails:** (1) decouple "merge proceeds" from "cert pass recorded," but guarantee every merged change *eventually* gets its recorded certified pass — that keeps it policy-compliant. (2) Instrument preemption rate via the per-step JSON logs before/after, so you can confirm p stays well under the 60% break-even.

RECOMMENDATION: Move the integration suite off the blocking lane onto the preemptible background lane (~40% billing rate) while still recording a certified full-suite pass asynchronously for every merged change.

**The cost driver is the integration suite, and the amplifier is its 2.6× auto-retry — but that amplifier is structurally unfixable, so the right lever attacks the *price* of that compute, not its *quantity*.**

Walking the data against Conveyor's architecture:

**Where the money is.** The integration suite is the single largest compute slice. Every merged change triggers a full run (compliance requires a certified full-suite pass per merge), and each run averages **2.6 attempts** before it passes. That 2.6× multiplier is exactly why compute has decoupled from commit volume: cost scales with *attempts*, not commits, and flakiness inflates attempts independent of how many commits land. Fixture-build caching already shipped in Q1 — rebuild is down to ~6% of runtime — so the remaining spend is the conformance tests themselves, executed 2.6× over.

**Why you can't cut the quantity.** Every lever that would reduce *attempts* is foreclosed:
- The flaky tests live in a **vendored, pinned** conformance suite — you can't patch them.
- The runner is **all-or-nothing** with no per-test rerun API — a failure re-executes the whole suite.
- A **quarantine lane** for the flaky subset doesn't help, because the certification report requires a *fresh full-suite pass on every run* — partial reruns don't count.
- **Nightly runs or merge sampling** don't satisfy the policy either (certified full-suite pass required for *every* merged change).

So the 2.6× is locked in. The base volume can't shrink.

**The highest-leverage fix: move the integration suite to the preemptible background lane.**

This is the best lever because it's the one reduction that's both *available* and *aimed at the biggest slice*:

1. **It discounts the largest cost slice by ~60%** — preemptible is billed at ~40% of the blocking rate.
2. **The unfixable retry multiplier makes the discount *more* valuable, not less.** You're applying 60% off to 2.6× the base volume — the amplification you can't remove is precisely what makes cutting the *rate* pay off so heavily.
3. **It's policy-safe.** The compliance policy requires a *recorded* certified full-suite pass per merge but explicitly does **not** require that run to block the merge. The preemptible lane still produces and records a full certified pass — it just stops gating.
4. **Nothing downstream gates on completion**, so moving it off the critical path costs nothing in throughput. Preemption-induced restarts (a real risk given the all-or-nothing runner) are also billed at 40% and, since latency no longer blocks merges, are effectively free where they used to be blocking-lane minutes.

**Why the runners-up lose:** fixture caching is already done (6% left — nearly tapped out); unit shards are a minor, stable slice; quarantine and partial reruns are killed by the full-pass certification rule; nightly/sampling violates the per-merge policy. The lane move is the only lever that touches the dominant slice without hitting a policy or vendor wall.

Every commit therefore buys ~2.6 executions of your most expensive job — so spend scales as `commits × 2.6 × (suite cost)`, which climbs far faster than commit count alone. That 2.6× amplifier on the biggest slice is precisely why the bill decoupled from commit volume.

**But the multiplier itself is immovable.** The instinct is "fix the flakiness / stop the retries." Every path there is foreclosed by hard constraints:
- Flaky tests live in a **vendored conformance suite pinned upstream** — you can't patch them.
- The runner is **all-or-nothing** with no per-test rerun API — a failure re-executes the whole suite.
- A **quarantine lane doesn't count** — certification requires a fresh full-suite pass every run.
- **Fixture caching already shipped** (Q1) — rebuild is down to ~6%, so that well is dry.
- Unit shards are a minority slice and stable — not where the money is.

So the retry lever has high theoretical impact but ~zero feasibility. **Leverage = impact × feasibility**, and on that basis it scores near zero.

## The highest-leverage fix

**Move the integration suite off the blocking lane onto the preemptible background lane.**

- Preemptible capacity bills at **~40% of the blocking rate → a 60% price cut** on your single largest slice.
- Critically, that discount applies to the *entire* multiplied cost. You still pay 2.6 attempts, but each attempt now costs 40%. You don't need to defeat the multiplier — you cut the unit price underneath it.
- If the integration suite is (as stated) the largest slice and it's inflated 2.6×, it plausibly dominates the bill; a 60% cut on it removes a very large fraction of total compute in one change — an order of magnitude more than any other available lever can touch.

**Why it's unblocked where everything else is blocked:** the compliance policy requires a certified full-suite pass *recorded* for every merged change — but explicitly **does not require that run to block the merge**, and nothing downstream gates on the suite finishing. That single clause is what makes the move legal: you keep running the full suite on every change (satisfying certification), you just record the pass asynchronously off the critical path instead of holding a blocking runner. Nightly/sampling would violate the policy; this doesn't, because it's still a full pass per change.

## The risk to watch

Preemptible capacity can be evicted, and an all-or-nothing suite with no partial rerun must restart from scratch on eviction — which could inflate the effective attempt count beyond 2.6. Two things contain it: (1) nothing gates on completion, so eviction costs latency, not correctness; and (2) at a 40% rate, restarts would have to more than **2.5×** total compute to erase the savings — implausible for a background lane. Instrument the per-step JSON logs to track effective attempts after the move and confirm eviction overhead stays well under that buffer.

**Net:** the retry multiplier explains the runaway bill but can't be removed; the best move is to stop paying blocking-lane prices for it.
